behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real filesystem watching behavior (recursive watch coverage and OS event delivery from nested paths). A mock watcher can hide backend-specific gaps, so REAL-INFRA and MOCK-HIDE push this to System. Rejected higher levels: behavior text is only filesystem processing; no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent sequence, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon against a temp input root and real output dir, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert exactly one transformed output is produced for that nested file path within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily module handoff/state behavior: request intake + dedupe window store + dispatch suppression. It does not require real OS/network/subprocess to reproduce; two in-process requests with controlled timing can expose the bug. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real infra required, (b) no AI/ML output, (c) no multi-agent chain, (d) no LLM orchestration. Rejected higher level (System): behavior text is about duplicate processing requests and time-window logic, not OS-specific event semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real intake/deduper/dispatcher modules in-process with a controllable clock; submit two requests for the same path inside the configured window and assert only one dispatch occurs, then submit after window expiry and assert dispatch resumes.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly depends on OS-emitted duplicate filesystem events. Real watcher backend behavior (coalescing/burst patterns) is the failure boundary, so mocks risk false confidence. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML content judgment and no multi-step agent pipeline in the behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., temp-write + rename or chunked append), and assert one and only one pipeline execution/output for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS/filesystem lifecycle scenario (directory deletion, inode replacement, watcher reattachment) that only appears with real filesystem events and watcher backend behavior. In-process fakes can miss the true failure. Rejected higher levels: behavior text contains no AI/ML decision dependency and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp directory, remove the watched directory while running, recreate it at the same path, write a new file, and verify processing resumes without restarting the daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering here depends on filesystem timestamp metadata and near-simultaneous arrival behavior, which can diverge from in-process assumptions due to timestamp resolution and event timing. Real filesystem interaction is needed to catch practical ordering failures. Rejected higher levels: no AI/ML output dependency and no multi-agent pipeline in this behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: With the real daemon and filesystem, create several files in rapid succession (ensuring distinguishable creation times when possible), capture processing order, and assert it matches creation-timestamp order; include a tie-handling assertion if timestamps collide.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real I/O timing and file-stability boundary (partial writes, close/flush semantics, event timing). Mocked writes often hide these races, so System is the minimum to catch real failures. Rejected higher levels: behavior text does not involve AI/ML output quality or multi-agent sequencing.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on a real directory, write a large file in chunks with delays so arrival event occurs mid-write, assert no processing starts before final chunk/close, then assert processing occurs once after completion.
